mnemonics.title: Critical Section Requirements
mnemonics.content: MUTUAL EXCLUSION: ONLY ONE CAN ENTER. PROGRESS: NOBODY IN REMAINDER CAN DECIDE. BOUNDED WAITING: LIMITED RE-ENTRANTS.

mnemonics.title: Deadlock Conditions
mnemonics.content: MUTUAL EXCLUSION: RESOURCE IS EXCLUSIVE. HOLD AND WAIT: KEEPS HOLDING WHILE WAITING. NO PREEMPTION: CANNOT TAKE BACK RESOURCES. CIRCULAR WAIT: CHAIN OF PROCESSES WAITING.

mnemonics.title: Producer-Consumer Semaphores
mnemonics.content: EMPTY COUNTS EMPTY SLOTS. FULL COUNTS FILLED SLOTS. EMPTY STARTS AT N (BUFFER SIZE), FULL STARTS AT 0.

acronyms.usage: The fundamental problem of coordinating access to shared resources in multi-process systems
Dutch terms for wait and signal operations on semaphores
Scheduling method that can be applied to critical section entry
A synchronization mechanism for read-heavy workloads
Atomic instruction used for synchronization in modern processors

visualTips.visualization: Imagine two people (P0 and P1) with a door (turn) and two flags. One person raises their flag and sets turn to the other. They wait while the other's flag is up and it's their turn.
Think of a semaphore as a counting token system. wait() takes a token, signal() returns a token. If no tokens, you must wait.
Five people at a circular table with a fork between each. They need two forks to eat. Each picks up the left fork then right fork - creates deadlock if all pick up left simultaneously.
A monitor is like a single-person bathroom. Only one person can enter at a time. Others must wait outside until the occupant leaves.

analogies.analogy: Like a single bathroom in a house - only one person can use it at a time to avoid conflicts.
Like a movie theater with limited seats. The semaphore counts available seats. When full, new customers wait outside.
Like repeatedly calling someone to ask if they're done, instead of doing other work. Wasteful but fast if the task is very short.
Two people each holding one resource and waiting for the other's resource - like two people each holding one chopstick in the dining philosophers problem.
